Join The Attachment Code
The Attachment Code
Dismissive Avoidant Journal
Understanding Your Pattern

You are not cold. You are not broken. You are someone whose nervous system learned, very early, that depending on others was a dangerous gamble — and built every possible defence against that risk. This is a private space to begin understanding that, with honesty, with compassion, and without the story that your independence is the problem.

What this journal is

A private space to understand the dismissive avoidant pattern — not to fix yourself, but to finally see yourself clearly. Every entry lives only on your device.

Speak or write

Every response field has a microphone button. Tap it to speak your answer — your words transcribe in real time. Speaking often reveals more than writing. Both are valid.

Private by design

Your entries are saved locally on this device only. No server. No database. No one but you can access what you write here.

Your Attachment Scan

Move the sliders to check in with where you are today.
Choose Your Portal
The Wall
Shutdown · Defence
The automatic emotional perimeter that goes up before you've even decided to pull back.
The Shutdown
Deactivation · Exit
The pattern of switching off when things get serious. Finding reasons to leave before they can leave you.
The Cost
Dismissal · Loss
What the pattern of minimising — others and yourself — has actually taken from your life and relationships.
The Hunger
Longing · Beneath
The deep, unnamed loneliness underneath the independence. What you actually want and have learned to hide.
The Original Wound
Origin · History
Where the self-sufficiency was first required. The earliest moment depending on someone felt dangerous.
The Opening
Growth · Security
The daily choice to let the guard down by an inch. Vulnerability as courage, not as weakness.

Daily Practice

Seven steps. Work through them in order, or drop into wherever feels most true today.

1
Arrival
Before anything else, check in with your body. Is there a heaviness in your chest? A desire to be somewhere else? A flatness you can't quite name? Just notice — without fixing.
Flat Numb Suffocated Restless Detached Heavy Irritable Grounded Present
2
The Trigger
Did anything activate your shutdown or withdrawal today? A demand, a closeness, a conversation that felt like too much? Describe it without judging yourself for the response.
3
The Story
What interpretation did your mind place on what happened? Not the facts — the conclusion you drew. About them, about the relationship, about what it meant.
4
The Origin
Is this familiar? Does this situation remind you of something earlier — a feeling of being trapped, overwhelmed, or let down? How old do you feel in this response?
5
The Truth
Strip the story back. What actually happened — just the observable facts? What did you observe versus what did your mind add? Is the shutdown proportionate to what actually occurred?
6
The Choice
What is one small thing you can do today that moves toward connection rather than away from it? It doesn't have to be large. It just has to be honest.
7
The Closing
One honest sentence about where you actually are today. Not where you think you should be. Where you are.

Private by design. Your entries are saved locally on this device only. No one else can access them.

Guided Prompts

Tell the guide what you're working through. She'll create personalised prompts, practices, or exercises written specifically for where you are right now — in this relationship, in this pattern, in this moment.

Understanding what triggered the shutdown
Working through the urge to withdraw
Sitting with closeness without switching off
Understanding a dismissal pattern I keep repeating
Separating what I observed from what I concluded
Processing the cost of keeping people at distance
Connecting current patterns to earlier experiences
Writing to someone I shut out or walked away from

this week

Weekly Reflection

What was the strongest dismissive avoidant activation this week? What triggered it and what did you do?

Where Did You Stay?

Where did you manage to stay present or connected this week, even when the urge to withdraw was there?

One Inch Forward

What is one moment this week where you let someone in, even slightly? However small.

This Week's Actions
Complete your daily 7-step practice
Identify one shutdown and name what it was actually protecting you from
Share one true thing with someone you trust
Allow someone to help you with something without deflecting
Sit with a difficult feeling for five minutes before moving away from it
Write in one portal that you have been avoiding
Closing Ritual

You are not trying to become someone who needs people constantly. You are becoming someone who can choose connection without losing themselves in it. Every time you notice the shutdown and stay anyway — even once, even for a moment — you are changing the pattern. That is not small. That is everything.
